corrupted_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Hybrid CNN-LSTM vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Cohort Definition

The analysis uses structured EHR data from 10,000 chronic disease patients spanning 5 years. Each observation represents a single index discharge event with a 30-day follow-up window and a 180-day look-back period for feature construction. The binary readmission label is defined as any unplanned hospital readmission within 30 days of discharge (standard clinical definition). Elective and scheduled procedures are excluded from the readmission definition.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To respect causal ordering and prevent information leakage, the data is divided by discharge date into three sequential windows: (1) Training: discharges from years 1–3 (~6,000 patients); (2) Validation: discharges from year 4, first half (~1,500 patients); (3) Test: discharges from year 4, second half and year 5 (~2,500 patients). Within each window, the cohort is stratified by readmission class (0/1) to maintain class balance representation. This temporal split reflects deployment reality—a model trained on historical discharges is applied to prospective cohorts. The stratified component ensures that class imbalance (anticipated at 15–25% readmission rate) does not bias any split.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Features are constructed from the 180-day look-back window prior to discharge and encode four dimensions: (1) Demographics and baseline risk (age, comorbidity index); (2) Temporal utilization patterns (prior admission counts in rolling 30/60/90-day windows, time since last admission, admission frequency stability); (3) Index admission characteristics (length of stay, admission source, primary diagnosis); (4) Medication and clinical instability (medication burden, recent abnormal labs, vital sign variability). Temporal features explicitly capture sequence structure and irregular visit intervals, enabling the LSTM to learn patterns of clinical deterioration.

All preprocessing is fit on the training set only: mean imputation for missing values, z-score standardization for continuous features, and one-hot encoding for categorical variables (fitted on training vocabulary). These transformations are applied identically to validation and test sets, preventing leakage and ensuring honest out-of-sample performance estimates.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The hybrid CNN-LSTM model processes temporal sequences: a 1D CNN (32–64 filters) extracts local patterns in visit dynamics, followed by LSTM layers (128 units) that capture long-range dependencies. The output passes through fully connected layers (64 units, ReLU) to a sigmoid output. Dropout (0.3) and L2 regularization (0.001) prevent overfitting.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on the identical feature set and preprocessed using the same fit-on-train approach. Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning on the validation set (learning rate, regularization strength, class weights). This ensures the comparison isolates the value of the temporal architecture, not differential tuning effort.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set using AUROC as the selection criterion. The optimal models (CNN-LSTM and logistic regression) are then frozen and evaluated once on the test set. No model selection, threshold tuning, or metric inspection occurs on the test set.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC). This metric reflects the operational goal—correctly ranking patients by readmission risk across the full spectrum to guide stratified intervention. AUROC is robust to class imbalance and avoids arbitrary threshold selection.

**Secondary metrics:** Precision-Recall AUC (more sensitive to class imbalance than AUROC), sensitivity at 90% specificity (ensures clinically safe false negative rates), and calibration (Expected Calibration Error—essential because readmission scores are communicated to clinicians). Subgroup analysis by age, comorbidity level, and admission source assesses generalization and detects disparities.

### 7. Addressing Confounding

Variable follow-up care intensity is addressed through feature inclusion (outpatient visit frequency, pharmacy refill patterns, care coordination flags) and subgroup stratification. The model learns associations between follow-up intensity and readmission but makes predictions conditional on observed care patterns—not counterfactual predictions. Subgroup performance is tracked; substantial degradation in low-follow-up groups signals uncontrolled confounding. Results are scoped as predictive accuracy given current care practices, not causal estimates of readmission determinants.

### 8. Success Criteria

The hypothesis is supported if the CNN-LSTM achieves meaningfully higher validation and test AUROC than logistic regression (e.g., ≥0.03 absolute improvement), with comparable or superior calibration and sensitivity, and consistent subgroup performance across follow-up intensity strata.
